concepts.title: Direction Ratio
concepts.conceptBody:
Direction Ratios and Directions Cosines

Let's start with an example to easily understand:

If $\frac{2}{3},-\frac{2}{3},\frac{1}{3}$ are direction cosines of a vector $\vec{r}$, then its direction ratios are $2,-2,1$ or $-2,2,-1$ or $4,-4,2$, because

$\begin{array}{l} \frac{2/3}{2} =\frac{-2/3}{2}=\frac{1/3}{1},\frac{2/3}{-2}=\frac{-2/3}{2} \\ =\frac{1/3}{-1},\frac{2/3}{4}=\frac{-2/3}{-4}=\frac{1/3}{2}. \\ \end{array}$

It is evident from the above definition that to obtain direction ratios of a vector from its direction cosines we just multiply them by a common number. This also shows that there can be infinitely many sets of direction ratios for a given vector. But the direction cosines are unique.

Obtaining Direction Cosines (DC) from Direction Ratios (DR)

Let a,b,c be direction ratios of a vector $\vec{r}$ having direction cosines $1,m,n$. Then,

$\frac{\text{l}}{\text{a}}=\frac{\text{m}}{\text{b}}=\frac{\text{n}}{\text{c}}$

Let $\frac{\text{l}}{\text{a}}=\frac{\text{m}}{\text{b}}=\frac{\text{n}}{\text{c}}=\lambda $. Then $\text{l}=\text{a}\lambda ,\text{m}=\text{b}\lambda ,\text{n}=\text{c}\lambda $

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & \text{ }{{\text{l}}^{2}}+{{\text{m}}^{2}}+{{\text{n}}^{2}}=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \text{ }{{\text{a}}^{2}}{{\lambda }^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}{{\lambda }^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}{{\lambda }^{2}}=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda =\pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}} \\ \end{array}$

So, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }l=\pm \frac{a}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}+{{c}^{2}}}},m=\pm \frac{b}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}+{{c}^{2}}}},n=\pm \frac{c}{\sqrt{{{a}^{2}}+{{b}^{2}}+{{c}^{2}}}}$

where the signs should be taken all positive or all negative.

Thus, if a,b,c are direction ratios of a vector, then its direction cosines are given by

$\pm \frac{\text{a}}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}},\pm \frac{\text{b}}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}},\pm \frac{\text{c}}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}}$

where the signs should be taken all positive or all negative.

For example, if $3,-4,12$ are direction ratios of a vector then its direction cosines are

$\frac{3}{\sqrt{{{(3)}^{2}}+{{(-4)}^{2}}+{{(12)}^{2}}}},\frac{-4}{\sqrt{{{(3)}^{2}}+{{(-4)}^{2}}+{{(12)}^{2}}}},\frac{12}{\sqrt{{{(3)}^{2}}+{{(-4)}^{2}}+{{(12)}^{2}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{3}{13},-\frac{4}{13},\frac{12}{13}$

concepts.title: DR of Line
concepts.conceptBody:
Some Other Result

The DC's & DR's for a given point $r$ 

if $\vec{r}=\text{a}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+\text{b}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\,+\text{c}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\,$

then the Direction Ratios are given by a,b & c only where the Direction Cosines $\left( \text{l},\text{m},\text{n} \right)$ are given by

$\text{l}=\frac{\text{a}}{\left| \text{r} \right|},\text{m}=\frac{\text{b}}{\left| {\vec{r}} \right|},\text{n}=\frac{\text{c}}{\left| {\vec{r}} \right|}$

If a vector $\vec{r}$ has direction ratios a,b,c then

$\vec{r}=\frac{\left| {\vec{r}} \right|}{\sqrt{{{\text{a}}^{2}}+{{\text{b}}^{2}}+{{\text{c}}^{2}}}}\left( \text{ai}+\text{b}\overset{}{\mathop{j}}\,+\text{ck} \right)$

Direction Ratio of a Line 

If $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)~\And~ \text{Q}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right)$ are points given which have to form the line, then the

DRs of $PQ$ are $\left( {{x}_{2}}-{{x}_{1}} \right),\left( {{y}_{2}}-{{y}_{1}} \right),\left( {{z}_{2}}-{{z}_{1}} \right)$

DC's of $\text{PQ}$ are $\frac{\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)}{\left| \overline{\text{PQ}} \right|},\frac{{{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{\left| \overline{\text{PQ}} \right|},\frac{\left( {{\text{z}}_{2}}-{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)}{\overline{\text{PQ}}}$

NOTE : 

  • The DC's of a line are defined as the direction cosines of any vector whose support is the given line.
  • If $1,m,n$ are DC of a given line, then $-1,-m,-n$ are also the DC's of the same line.

Case of Parallel lines / vectors 

If $\bar{a}~\And~ \bar{b}$ are 2 parallel vectors then they have same DR & DC.

Projection of a segment on a line 

The projection of line segment joining points $\text{P}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}},{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)~\And~ \text{Q}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}},{{\text{y}}_{2}},{{\text{z}}_{2}} \right)$ on a line with DC's $1,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m},\text{n}$ is

$\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\text{l}+\left( {{\text{y}}_{2}}-{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)\text{m}+\left( {{\text{z}}_{2}}-{{\text{z}}_{1}} \right)\text{n}$

DC's of angle bisector 

If $\left( {{\text{l}}_{1}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{1}},{{\text{n}}_{1}} \right)~\And~ \left( {{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}_{2}},{{\text{n}}_{2}} \right)$ are DCs of two concurrent lines, then the DC's of the lines bisecting the angle between them is given by

$\left( {{l}_{1}}\pm {{l}_{2}},{{m}_{1}}\pm {{m}_{2}},{{n}_{1}}\pm {{n}_{2}} \right).$
